elements.sent-2411-001: We prove that the average size of a mixed character sum \begin{equation}\sum_{1\le n \le x} \chi(n) e(n\theta) w(n/x)\end{equation} (for a suitable smooth function $w$) is on the order of $\sqrt{x}$ for all irrational real $\theta$ satisfying a weak Diophantine condition, where $\chi$ is drawn from the family of Dirichlet characters modulo a large prime $r$ and where $x\le r$.
elements.sent-2411-002: In contrast, it was proved by Harper that the average size is $o(\sqrt{x )$ for rational $\theta$.}
elements.sent-2411-003: Certain quadratic Diophantine equations play a key role in the present paper.
elements.sent-2411-004: Throughout this paper, let $r$ be a large prime.
elements.sent-2411-005: In a recent breakthrough work of Harper\cite{harpertypical , it is established that the typical size of Dirichlet character sum $\sum_{1\le n \le x \chi(n)$ is $o(\sqrt{x )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-006: Precisely, it is proved that if $1\le x\le r$ with $\min(x,r/x)\to \infty$, then \begin{equation}\label{harper} \frac{1}{r-1} \sum_{\chi \bmod r} |\sum_{1\le n \le x} \chi(n)| = o(\sqrt{x}),\end{equation} which would imply that for ``typical $\chi$", the character sum is of size $o(\sqrt{x )$.\footnote{In both the random model and the character sum cases, Harper proved a sharp quantitative saving.}}
elements.sent-2411-007: This somehow surprising phenomenon was first proved in an earlier beautiful work of Harper \cite{HarperLow in the random model case, namely instead of for a character $\chi$, the ``better than square-root cancellation" phenomenon holds for a random sum $\sum_{1\le n \le x f(n)$, where $f(n)$ is a random multiplicative function,\footnote{$f(p)$ is defined as an independent identically distributed random variable taking values on complex unit circle for all primes $p$, and $f(n)$ is defined as a completely multiplicative function.}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: In order to connect the deterministic case to the random multiplicative function model, Harper used a derandomization method.
elements.sent-2411-009: The underlying reason for the ``better than square-root cancellation" happening is subtle, which connects to the study of critical multiplicative chaos in probability theory.
elements.sent-2411-010: It is also believed that such a structure is delicate and may easily be destroyed by some perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-011: For example, one can consider general character sums like $\sum_{1\le n \le x a(n) \chi(n)$ (see \cite{harpertypical, WXratio ) or random sums $\sum_{1\le n \le x a(n)f(n)$ (see \cite{SoundXu, GWCLT, KSX ) with a twist $a(n)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: It is believed that for some generic coefficients $a(n)$, such a ``better than square-root cancellation" would die out (see \cite[\S 1]{Xu for more discussions).}
elements.sent-2411-013: In this paper, we are interested in mixed character sums of the form $\sum_{1\le n \le x \chi(n) e(n\theta)$, featuring both an additive character $e(n\theta)$ and a multiplicative character $\chi(n)$.}
elements.sent-2411-014: For technical reasons it is simpler for us to work with a smooth weight $w\maps\R\to \R$.
elements.sent-2411-015: Throughout this paper, fix a smooth function $w\maps \R\to \R$, supported on $[0,1]$, with $\int_0^1 w(t)^2\, dt > 0$.
elements.sent-2411-016: Note that by partial summation, \eqref{harper immediately implies \begin{equation}\frac{1}{r-1} \sum_{\chi \bmod r} |\sum_{n\ge 1} \chi(n) w(n/x)| = o(\sqrt{x}).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Similarly, it is also known that for any fixed rational number $\theta$ we have \begin{equation}\frac{1}{r-1} \sum_{\chi \bmod r} |\sum_{n\ge 1} \chi(n) e(n\theta) w(n/x) | = o(\sqrt{x}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-018: Our main theorem shows that the typical size will be on the square-root size when $\theta$ is not ``too close to the rationals".
elements.sent-2411-019: Let $\theta$ denote an irrational real number such that for some positive constant $C=C(\theta)$ and all $q \in {\mathbb N}$, the following holds: \begin{equation}\label{1.4} \Vert q \theta \Vert := \min_{n\in {\mathbb Z}} |q\theta -n | \ge C \exp( - q^{\frac{1}{4}}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-020: If $x$ is sufficiently large (in terms of $w$), then we have for all $ x\le r$, \begin{equation}\label{main-thm-goal} \sqrt{x} \ll \frac{1}{r-1}\sum_{\chi \bmod r} | \sum_{1\le n \le x} \chi(n) e(n\theta) w(n/x)| \ll \sqrt{x},\end{equation} where the implied constants are allowed to depend on $\theta$ (and $w$).
